title: Overfunding Kills Companies: The Lock Box Nobody Opens

source.original: Marc Andreessen: Will a16z Go Public & Why Labour Displacement with AI is Wrong (20VC)

body_html:
Andreessen claims a "zero percent" success rate at convincing founders not to take too much money. Despite this being "the one piece of startup advice that is tremendously grounded in reality for which everybody has many examples," no founder listens. The reason is psychological: accepting money is flattering.

The mechanism of death by overfunding is specific. Excess capital removes urgency. Without urgency, companies hire too fast (building teams before product-market fit), spend too aggressively on marketing (buying growth instead of earning it), and make commitment decisions that are hard to reverse (fancy offices, expensive tools, complicated organizational structures). Don Valentine's axiom: "More companies die from indigestion than from starvation."

The second killer is the valuation trap. Every round sets a price that becomes the hurdle for the next round. If you raise at a $500M valuation when you should have raised at $200M, you need to clear $500M+ to avoid a down round. And nobody wants to lead a down round in someone else's company — employees will hate you, other investors will hate you, the founders will hate you. So the company either clears the bar (forcing it to grow unsustainably fast) or dies trying to find the next investor willing to participate in the charade.

The "lock box" delusion is the final touch. Every founder who raises too much money says they'll put the excess in a lock box and only use it if needed. "No one has ever done the lock box. Nobody ever does the lock box." Money in the bank account gets spent. It's Parkinson's Law applied to capital: spending expands to fill the capital available.
